primary_diagnosis.name: Severe (chronic) mitral regurgitation with decompensated left ventricular failure and early pulmonary oedema

pathophysiology_summary: Chronic mitral regurgitation (MR) allows retrograde systolic flow from the left ventricle into the left atrium. For years the left ventricle compensates by dilating and becoming hyperdynamic (a laterally displaced, thrusting apex) to maintain forward output, and the enlarged left atrium accommodates the regurgitant volume; the murmur is a high-pitched pansystolic 'blowing' murmur loudest at the apex radiating to the axilla (the direction of the regurgitant jet), often with a soft S1 (incomplete valve closure) and an S3 (rapid diastolic filling of a volume-loaded ventricle). Eventually the ventricle can no longer compensate: left atrial and pulmonary venous pressures rise, producing exertional dyspnoea, orthopnoea, paroxysmal nocturnal cough and pulmonary congestion (bibasal crackles), while systemic venous congestion causes ankle oedema - i.e. decompensated left ventricular failure. Crucially, in chronic severe MR the ONSET of symptoms marks ventricular decompensation and is a key trigger for surgical valve repair/replacement, because the ejection fraction can appear preserved while the ventricle is already failing. Acute deterioration (as here, over two weeks) with resting tachypnoea/hypoxaemia signals early pulmonary oedema. Management is diuresis for congestion (with renal/electrolyte monitoring), afterload reduction, natriuretic peptides and, definitively, echocardiography to grade severity and cardiology/surgical referral for valve intervention. Auscultation of the murmur/apex/S3, safe diuretic administration and echocardiography cannot be delivered over telehealth, so urgent same-day in-person assessment is required.

differential.evidence_basis: Progressive exertional dyspnoea with new orthopnoea and ankle oedema suggests decompensating structural heart disease/heart failure.

differential.diagnosis: Other causes of heart failure (e.g. ischaemic/dilated cardiomyopathy)
differential.evidence_basis: The congestion syndrome could arise from other cardiac causes; echocardiography differentiates.
Breathlessness could be respiratory, but orthopnoea, ankle oedema and (sealed) cardiac signs point to a cardiac cause rather than airways disease.
Another valvular cause of exertional dyspnoea, distinguished on examination/echo (ejection murmur to carotids vs apical pansystolic to axilla).

differential_progression.scoring_note: The AI Doctor should recognise decompensated heart failure (likely valvular) and arrange urgent same-day assessment with echocardiography, not attribute it to deconditioning.

differential.diagnosis: Severe mitral regurgitation with decompensated LV failure
differential.evidence_basis: Progressive dyspnoea/fatigue (DI-001) + new orthopnoea/nocturnal cough (DI-002) + ankle oedema (DI-003), with resting tachypnoea/hypoxaemia and (sealed) apical pansystolic murmur to the axilla with a displaced hyperdynamic apex and S3, indicates decompensated severe MR.
Remains to be characterised on echo, but the murmur pattern points specifically to mitral regurgitation.
Orthopnoea, oedema and the structural cardiac signs are not a primary airways process.
The apical pansystolic murmur radiating to the axilla is mitral regurgitation, not the basal ejection murmur of aortic stenosis.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After the red-flag screen this is decompensated severe MR needing urgent same-day assessment, diuresis and echocardiography with cardiology/surgical referral.

differential.diagnosis: Severe mitral regurgitation with decompensated LV failure - presumptive, pending echocardiography and BNP
differential.evidence_basis: Working diagnosis for telehealth. Confirmation and severity grading require echocardiography; safe diuresis needs in-person monitoring, and valve surgery evaluation is the definitive pathway.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Final telehealth framing is decompensated severe MR/heart failure needing urgent same-day in-person assessment and echocardiography - not a remote fluid tablet or a next-week wait.

red_flags.description: Progressive exertional dyspnoea and severe fatigue, acutely worse, now limiting basic activities, with resting tachypnoea and SpO2 94%
red_flags.clinical_significance: Rapidly worsening functional decline with resting tachypnoea/hypoxaemia indicates significant decompensation and limited reserve.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Dismissed as deconditioning; congestion progresses to frank pulmonary oedema.

red_flags.description: New orthopnoea (three pillows) with nocturnal cough (pulmonary congestion)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Orthopnoea and nocturnal cough reflect raised pulmonary venous pressure and early pulmonary oedema, marking decompensation.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A nocturnal episode escalates to acute pulmonary oedema without timely response.

red_flags.description: Apical pansystolic blowing murmur radiating to the axilla, laterally displaced hyperdynamic apex, soft S1, S3 gallop, and bibasal crackles on clinician examination
red_flags.clinical_significance: These clinician-elicited signs establish severe mitral regurgitation with volume overload and pulmonary congestion; auscultation and apex assessment cannot be done over telehealth.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Not applicable to telehealth - requires clinician examination; the correct action is urgent in-person assessment.

red_flags.description: Severe regurgitant jet and LV dilatation/dysfunction on echocardiography - pending
red_flags.clinical_significance: Echocardiography grades MR severity and LV function and determines the need/timing of valve surgery; it cannot be obtained via telehealth.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Not applicable to telehealth - imaging requires an in-person service; the correct action is urgent referral.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Progressive dyspnoea with orthopnoea and oedema over a short period is decompensation, not simple deconditioning, and warrants urgent assessment.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Not recognising symptom-onset as a surgical trigger in chronic MR
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: In chronic severe MR the onset of symptoms signals ventricular decompensation and usually mandates valve surgery evaluation.
Diuresis requires renal/electrolyte monitoring and in-person assessment; a remote fluid tablet without follow-up is unsafe.
Orthopnoea, oedema and the cardiac signs indicate a cardiac cause, not primary airways disease.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Cardiac auscultation for the apical pansystolic murmur, soft S1, and S3
Assessment of the apex beat (displaced, hyperdynamic)
Accurate clinician-measured SpO2, respiratory rate and blood pressure
Echocardiography to grade MR severity and LV function
Natriuretic peptides, renal function/electrolytes to guide safe diuresis
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Urgent same-day in-person assessment (emergency department or acute medical/rapid-access cardiology) for oxygen if needed, monitored diuresis with renal/electrolyte checks, ECG, chest X-ray, natriuretic peptides and echocardiography, and cardiology/surgical referral - with an ambulance (000) if breathing suddenly worsens at rest, pink frothy sputum appears, or chest pain develops.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Reassuring the patient it is deconditioning, or prescribing a water tablet remotely and deferring the scan to next week, risks progression to acute pulmonary oedema.
